elements.sent-2411-001: Test-time adaptation (TTA) of 3D point clouds is crucial for mitigating discrepancies between training and testing samples in real-world scenarios, particularly when handling corrupted point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-002: LiDAR data, for instance, can be affected by sensor failures or environmental factors, causing domain gaps.
elements.sent-2411-003: Adapting models to these distribution shifts online is crucial, as training for every possible variation is impractical.
elements.sent-2411-004: Existing methods often focus on fine-tuning pre-trained models based on self-supervised learning or pseudo-labeling, which can lead to forgetting valuable source domain knowledge over time and reduce generalization on future tests.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we introduce a novel 3D test-time adaptation method, termed 3DD-TTA, which stands for 3D Denoising Diffusion Test-Time Adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-006: This method uses a diffusion strategy that adapts input point cloud samples to the source domain while keeping the source model parameters intact.
elements.sent-2411-007: The approach uses a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to encode the corrupted point cloud into a shape latent and latent points.
elements.sent-2411-008: These latent points are corrupted with Gaussian noise and subjected to a denoising diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-009: During this process, both the shape latent and latent points are updated to preserve fidelity, guiding the denoising toward generating consistent samples that align more closely with the source domain.
elements.sent-2411-010: We conduct extensive experiments on the ShapeNet dataset and investigate its generalizability on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN, achieving state-of-the-art results.
elements.sent-2411-011: The code has been released at \url{https://github.com/hamidreza-dastmalchi/3DD-TTA .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figure/denoising_diagram10.pdf} \caption{Reconstruction of corrupted point clouds using the proposed 3DD-TTA method.} \label{fig:denoising_process_samples}
elements.sent-2411-012: Point cloud processing has recently gained much attention and is critical in various computer vision applications \cite{qi2017pointnet, pointnet++, xiang2021walk, zhang2023starting, hong2023pointcam .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Although significant progress has been made in this field \cite{qi2017pointnet, pointnet++, xiang2021walk, zhao2021point , much of the research has focused on controlled environments where the domain gap between training and testing samples is minimal.}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, real-world scenarios often feature test samples that deviate from the training domain.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, LiDAR point cloud data may be compromised by sensor failures or environmental factors, creating a domain gap that could lead to decreased performance.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, distribution shifts in 3D data can vary greatly, rendering it infeasible to train networks for every possible test-time variation in point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, it is crucial to efficiently adapt the models to unpredictable distribution shifts during the test stage in an online manner.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figure/motivation4.pdf} \caption{ In the TTA setting, the source model encounters corrupted 3D point clouds with an unknown distribution shift, requiring adaptation without prior knowledge of the corruption type. Our 3DD-TTA approach adds Gaussian noise to the latent points (encoded by a pre-trained VAE) to reduce their dependence on the initial corruption. The distribution of the Chamfer Distance (CD) between original and corrupted point clouds—corrupted with uniform noise, Gaussian noise, and RBF \cite{rbf}—from ShapeNet dataset \cite{shapenet} is shown before (Left) and after (Right) Gaussian perturbation. After perturbation, the CD distributions for all corruption types overlap, demonstrating corruption independence. } \label{fig:ood-independent}
elements.sent-2411-018: Current approaches for test-time adaptation often involve fine-tuning the parameters of a pre-trained source model during inference.
elements.sent-2411-019: Some methods~\cite{Wang_2024_WACV, dobler2022robust, tent utilize pseudo-labeling, adjusting the source model using pseudo-labels generated from test samples.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Other techniques~\cite{liu2021ttt++, fan2022self, mirza2023mate apply self-supervised learning to update the model for new test instances.}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, both strategies face a common challenge: they may initially perform well but risk forgetting valuable source domain knowledge over time.
elements.sent-2411-022: It promotes error accumulation during the long TTA process.
elements.sent-2411-023: This can diminish the model's generalization ability, leading to subpar performance on future test samples.
elements.sent-2411-024: Unlike previous TTA methods \cite{Wang_2024_WACV, dobler2022robust, tent, liu2021ttt++, fan2022self, mirza2023mate , which focus on updating the source model parameters and risk inducing forgetting, \cite{gao2023back, tsai2024gda, song2023target introduced an alternative approach: adapting input test images to the source domain using deep generative models, such as denoising diffusion models (DDM) \cite{ddpm, ddm_thermodynamics .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: While this approach has proven effective for 2D images \cite{gao2023back, tsai2024gda, song2023target, prabhudesai2023diffusion , applying it to 3D point clouds presents a far greater challenge due to the unstructured nature of point clouds and the inherent difficulty in preserving their fidelity during the denoising process.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Despite the growing success of diffusion models for 2D TTA, no existing method extends this powerful framework to 3D point clouds—until now.
elements.sent-2411-027: To this end, we introduce a novel, training-free test-time adaptation method called 3D Denoising Diffusion Test-Time Adaptation (3DD-TTA).
elements.sent-2411-028: By training-free, we mean that the method adapts corrupted point clouds without modifying or fine-tuning the network parameters during the test phase.
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, our method employs a latent diffusion model, initially trained on the source domain, to adapt corrupted point clouds back to the source domain (see Figure \ref{fig:denoising_process_samples ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: To enhance time efficiency, we reduced the computational overhead of our adaptation method by using a Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) \cite{ddim , which skips denoising steps without major performance loss.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Since point clouds typically lack high-frequency content, fewer denoising steps are sufficient to maintain performance.
elements.sent-2411-032: Before applying the reverse diffusion process in the latent space, we add Gaussian noise to the latent points (obtained from a VAE network) to reduce their dependence on variations in data distribution (see Figure \ref{fig:ood-independent ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: To guide the denoising process and ensure it does not generate an inconsistent point cloud, we employ a modified Chamfer distance, termed Selective Chamfer Distance (SCD).
elements.sent-2411-034: The SCD measures the discrepancy between the original and predicted latent points at each time step, with updates made based on the gradient of this distance.
elements.sent-2411-035: This method improves fidelity while accommodating variations in the latent points.
elements.sent-2411-036: Ultimately, we obtain a refined point cloud that is better aligned with the source domain, preserving the essential characteristics necessary for accurate classification.
elements.sent-2411-037: This alignment allows the source classifier to leverage its pre-trained knowledge more effectively, resulting in improved accuracy and reliability during the classification process.
elements.sent-2411-038: In summary, our contributions are: {(1) The paper introduces a novel, training-free method called 3D Denoising Diffusion Test-Time Adaptation (3DD-TTA), which adapts corrupted point clouds rather than altering network parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-039: To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to employ denoising diffusion models for TTA of 3D point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-040: This model then denoises the perturbed latent points iteratively through the reverse process, effectively restoring their alignment with the source domain characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-041: The SCD allows for targeted updates to the latent points and shape latent, ensuring that fidelity is maintained while also permitting necessary variations.
elements.sent-2411-042: This mechanism improves the model’s adaptability in handling corrupted data, ensuring more accurate reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-043: Point Cloud Processing: PointNet~\cite{pointnet2017 , a pioneering work by Qi~\etal{ , marked the beginning of using multi-layer perceptron (MLP) networks for processing 3D point clouds.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, this initial method did not capture the intricate local structures inherent in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-045: To overcome this limitation, subsequent advancements like PointNet++~\cite{pointnet++2017 were introduced, employing hierarchical feature extraction to better capture local information.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Building on this, various studies~\cite{RS-CNN2019,spidercnn2018,SPHNet2019,SFCNN2019,pointconv2019,pointcnn2018, chowdhury2022few, ahmadi2024accv, C3PR_ECCV have proposed convolutional strategies to extract local information.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Concurrently, other research efforts~\cite{dgcnn2019,LocalSpecGCN2018,PointGCN2018 have represented point clouds as graph vertices, enabling feature extraction in spatial or spectral domains.}
elements.sent-2411-048: For instance, DGCNN~\cite{dgcnn2019 constructs graphs in feature space and dynamically updates them using MLPs for each edge.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In contrast, PointGCN~\cite{PointGCN2018 employs k-nearest neighbors to generate graphs directly from point clouds, efficiently capturing local information.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Diffusion Models: Denoising Diffusion Models (DDMs) \cite{ddpm, ddm_thermodynamics are a class of generative models that synthesize data by systematically reversing the forward diffusion process.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This reverse process effectively transforms noisy inputs into high-quality outputs that align closely with the original data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-052: DDMs are especially noted for their ability to generate intricate images and complex data forms~\cite{glide, dalle2, imagen, latent-diffusion-model, sdxl-turbo .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Applying generative models to point clouds provides a powerful framework for unsupervised representation learning, effectively capturing data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, the development of diffusion models for 3D point clouds lags behind their image counterparts, largely due to the challenges posed by the irregular sampling patterns of point clouds in 3D space \cite{3d-diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, a few diffusion models have been introduced for 3D point clouds \cite{3d-diffusion-gradient, point-voxel-3d-diffusion, 3d-diffusion, lion .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The LION model \cite{lion distinguishes itself by applying a diffusion model to a hierarchical latent space, combining the global shape latent and local latent points to enhance expressivity and performance.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We leveraged this latent diffusion model for the TTA task, utilizing its hierarchical framework to effectively adapt 3D point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-058: Test-Time Adaptation: Test-time adaptation (TTA) adapts a source-trained model to a new target domain during inference without access to the source data.
elements.sent-2411-059: Common strategies include fine-tuning with an unsupervised loss on unlabeled target samples, generally designed for image domain adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-060: TTT~\cite{sun2020test uses a self-supervised proxy task, while TENT~\cite{wang2021tent updates batch normalization parameters by minimizing model response entropy.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: TTT++~\cite{liu2021ttt++ adds a self-supervised branch with contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-063: MM-TTA~\cite{shin2022mm merges predictions from multiple modalities using intra- and inter-modules for pseudo-labels.}
elements.sent-2411-064: DSS~\cite{Wang_2024_WACV addresses noisy pseudo-labels with dynamic thresholding and learning processes.}
elements.sent-2411-065: For 3D point clouds, several TTA methods are specifically designed.
elements.sent-2411-066: MATE~\cite{mirza2023mate employs masked autoencoding for robustness to distribution shifts in 3D point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Hatem et al.~\cite{hatem2023test uses meta-learning for point cloud upsampling.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Point-TTA~\cite{hatem2023point adapts model parameters instance-specifically during inference for point cloud registration.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In contrast to the fine-tuning methods, the authors in \cite{gao2023back proposed a data-driven approach for TTA of 2D images using DDMs.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Similarly, \cite{tsai2024gda, song2023target employed diffusion models with structural guidance to enhance generalization for 2D image adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this work, we introduce a novel approach for TTA in 3D point clouds, leveraging a latent diffusion model with 3D shape guidance to address different distribution shifts.
elements.sent-2411-072: Problem Formulation: Consider corrupted point clouds, each containing \(n\) points \(\tilde{x \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times 3}\), belonging to a target domain \(\mathcal{Q _t\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: We have a classifier \(p_c\) and a diffusion model \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon _{\theta}\), both trained on original point clouds \(x \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times 3}\) from a source domain \(\mathcal{Q _s\), where a domain gap exists between \(\mathcal{Q _t\) and \(\mathcal{Q _s\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The goal during test-time adaptation is to improve classifier accuracy on the corrupted data by adapting it back to \(\mathcal{Q _s\) using \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon _{\theta}\), without accessing the source data or modifying the classifier \(p_c\).}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Denoising Diffusion Models: Denoising diffusion models (DDMs) \cite{ddpm, ddm_thermodynamics are generative models that learn the data distribution \( q(x) \) by reversing a forward diffusion process.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In this process, Gaussian noise is progressively added to the data in a Markovian manner, and the model is trained to recover the original data from this noisy sequence.
elements.sent-2411-077: The forward diffusion process is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} q(x_{1:T} | x_0) := \prod_{t=1}^{T} q(x_t | x_{t-1}), q(x_t | x_{t-1}) := \mathcal{N}\left(x_t; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}x_{t-1}, \beta_t I\right) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, \( T \) denotes the number of diffusion steps.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} q(x_{1:T} | x_0) := \prod_{t=1}^{T} q(x_t | x_{t-1}), q(x_t | x_{t-1}) := N\left(x_t; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}x_{t-1}, \beta_t I\right) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: The choice of \( \beta_t \) aims to ensure that the chain approximately converges to a standard Gaussian distribution after \( T \) steps, i.e., \( q(x_T) \approx \mathcal{N (0, I) \).}
elements.sent-2411-079: According to the Markov chain property, for any intermediate timestep \( t \), we have: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} q(x_t | x_0) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_t; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}x_0, (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t)I\right), x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \end{aligned} \label{eq:direct-forward-diffusion}\end{equation} where \( \bar{\alpha _t := \prod_{s=1 ^{t (1 - \beta_s) \) and \( \boldsymbol{\epsilon }\) is drawn from a standard Gaussian distribution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Similarly, the generative process is modeled as a Gaussian transition with a learned mean \( \boldsymbol{\mu _\theta \): \begin{equation}p_\theta(x_{t-1} | x_t) = \mathcal{N}\left(x_{t-1}; \boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta(x_t, t), \sigma_t^2 I\right)\end{equation} Here, \( \sigma_t \) is typically a predefined constant associated with the variance schedule, and \( \boldsymbol{\mu _\theta(x_t, t) \) is parameterized by a denoising U-Net, denoted by \( \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \), with the following equivalence \cite{ddpm : \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta(x_t, t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \left(x_t - \frac{1 - \alpha_t}{\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}} \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\right)\end{equation} The denoising network is trained by minimizing the variational upper bound on the negative log-likelihood of the data $x_0$ under $p_\theta(x_{0:T )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: This objective can be succinctly expressed as: \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{t \sim U\{1,T\}, x_0 \sim p(x_0), x_t \sim q(x_t|x_0)} \left[ \frac{1}{2} || \boldsymbol{\epsilon} - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)||^2_2 \right]\end{equation} Latent Diffusion Model for 3D Point Cloud: In this study, we employ the hierarchical latent diffusion model (LION) proposed in \cite{lion as the backbone for the TTA task.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\min_{\theta} E_{t \sim U\{1,T\}, x_0 \sim p(x_0), x_t \sim q(x_t|x_0)} \left[ \frac{1}{2} || \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)||^2_2 \right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: The LION model leverages a VAE network composed of two hierarchical encoders and one decoder.
elements.sent-2411-083: The first encoder, denoted as \(q_{z (z_0|x)\), converts the input point cloud \(x\) into an abstract latent vector \(z_{0}\in \mathbb{R ^{D_z}\), referred to as the shape latent.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Subsequently, another encoder, denoted as \(q_{h (h_0|z_0, x)\), maps the input point cloud to a point cloud-structured latent space, represented by \(h_0\in \mathbb{R ^{4\times n}\), referred to as the latent points.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Here, the first three dimensions represent the $xyz$-coordinates, and the last dimension represents an additional feature.
elements.sent-2411-086: Additionally, two denoising diffusion networks denoted by $\boldsymbol{\epsilon _z\left(z_t, t\right)$ and $\boldsymbol{\epsilon _h\left(\mathbf{h _t, z_0, t\right)$ are trained to model the shape latent and latent points, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Finally, the decoder denoted by \(p_{d (x|z_0, h_0)\) takes the shape latent and latent points as inputs and maps them back to the point cloud.}
elements.sent-2411-088: This generative framework is trained in two stages.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the first stage, the encoders and the decoder are simultaneously trained to maximize the variational lower bound over the data log-likelihood: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{ p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right), q_{h}\left(\mathbf{h}_0 \mid x, z_0\right)} \Big[ \log p_{d}\left(x \mid z_0, \mathbf{h}_0\right) \quad - \gamma_{z} D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right) \mid p\left(z_0\right)\right) \quad - \gamma_{\mathbf{h}} D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q_{h}\left(\mathbf{h}_0 \mid z_0, x\right) \mid p\left(\mathbf{h}_0\right)\right) \Big] \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, $p(z_0)$ and $p(h_0)$ denote the Gaussian priors imposed on shape latent and latent points, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} L_{ELBO} = E_{ p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right), q_{h}\left(h_0 \mid x, z_0\right)} \Big[ \log p_{d}\left(x \mid z_0, h_0\right) \quad - \gamma_{z} D_{KL}\left(q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right) \mid p\left(z_0\right)\right) \quad - \gamma_{h} D_{KL}\left(q_{h}\left(h_0 \mid z_0, x\right) \mid p\left(h_0\right)\right) \Big] \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-090: In the second stage, the two latent diffusion models are trained on the encodings $z_0$ and $h_0$ sampled from $q_{z (z_0|x)$ and $q_{h (h_0|z_0, x)$, minimizing the following loss functions: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM}_z}=\mathbb{E}_{t, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right) }\left\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_z\left(z_t, t\right)\right\|_2^2, \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SM_h}}=\mathbb{E}_{t,\boldsymbol{\epsilon}, p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right), q_{h}\left(\mathbf{h}_0 \mid x, z_0\right)}\left\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{h}\left(\mathbf{h}_t, z_0, t\right)\right\|_2^2 \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} L_{SM_z}=E_{t, \epsilon, p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right) }\left\|\epsilon-\epsilon_z\left(z_t, t\right)\right\|_2^2, L_{SM_h}=E_{t,\epsilon, p(x), q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid x\right), q_{h}\left(h_0 \mid x, z_0\right)}\left\|\epsilon-\epsilon_{h}\left(h_t, z_0, t\right)\right\|_2^2 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: We provide an overview of the proposed diffusion-based TTA method, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:overview-diagram .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Starting with a corrupted test point cloud \(\tilde{x}\) from domain \(\mathcal{Q _t\), our objective is to adapt the data back to the source domain \(\mathcal{Q _s\) to improve the performance of a classifier \(p_c\) trained on \(\mathcal{Q _s\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Leveraging the LION model \cite{lion , the input point cloud \(\tilde{x}\) is first encoded into a shape latent and latent points, represented as \(z_0\) and \(h_0\), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The latent points \(h_0\) are then perturbed with Gaussian noise \(\epsilon \in \mathcal{N (0, I)\) at the \(t_w\) time step using Eq. \ref{eq:direct-forward-diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The noisy latent points undergo \(t_w\) iterations of denoising through the diffusion network until they sufficiently revert to resemble the source domain point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-096: The denoising process is guided by minimizing our proposed Selective Chamfer Distance (SCD), between the predicted and the original latent points to enhance fidelity, which is denoted by \(l^{\lambda _{cd}\), where \(\lambda\) is a hyperparameter controlling the strictness of the similarity enforcement.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In addition, the shape latent \(z_0\) is updated in the direction of the SCD gradient to improve the conditional signal it provides to the diffusion model during the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, after $t_w$ steps of denoising, the rectified latent points $h_0^r$ are decoded to generate the adapted point cloud.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/blockdiagram-v12.pdf} \caption{Given a corrupted test point cloud \(\tilde{x}\), we adapt it to the source domain to improve classifier \(p_c\). Using the LION model \cite{lion}, the point cloud is encoded into shape latent \(z_0\) and latent points \(h_0\). The latent points \(h_0\), perturbed with Gaussian noise, are progressively denoised over \(t_w\) iterations. During denoising, both shape latent and latent points are updated to minimize the SCD distance\(l^{\lambda}_{cd}\). After denoising, the latent points are decoded into the adapted point cloud and passed to the classifier \(p_c\) for prediction. } \label{fig:overview-diagram}
elements.sent-2411-099: Initially, the corrupted point cloud $\tilde{x}$ is decoded into the shape latent ${z _0$ and latent points ${h _0$ using the corresponding encoders, $q_z(.)$ and $q_h(.)$, respectively: \begin{equation}{z}_0 \sim q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid \tilde{x}\right), \quad {h}_0 \sim q_{h}\left(h_0 \mid {z}_0, \, \tilde{x} \right)\end{equation} To achieve effective test-time adaptation, the model must remain agnostic to various distribution shifts.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: To this end, we perturb the latent points with Gaussian noise through the $t_w \in (0,T)$ forward steps to neutralize the effects of different corruptions: \( {h _{t_w = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha _{t_w {h _{0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha _{t_w }\boldsymbol{\epsilon \), where ${h _{t_w}$ denotes the perturbed latent points and \( \boldsymbol{\epsilon }\) is drawn from a standard Gaussian distribution.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Following this, the added noise is estimated using the denoising network $\boldsymbol{\epsilon _h({h _{t_w , z_0, t)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Subsequently, the latent points of the previous time step ($h_{t_w-1}$) are estimated using the DDIM \cite{ddim sampling technique, guided by a regularization term denoted as $\mathcal{R}$: \begin{equation}{h}_{t_w-1} = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t_w-1}} \bar{h}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t_w-1}} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_h(h_{t_w}, z_0, t_w) + \eta \mathcal{R} \label{eq:latent_point_updating}\end{equation} The first two terms in Eq. \ref{eq:latent_point_updating constitute the non-markovian deterministic process in DDIM.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The regularization term aims to align the predicted latent points to the original ones.
elements.sent-2411-104: Employing this regularization term along with the deterministic denoising process ensures that the latent points are refined towards a coarse representation of original latent points.
elements.sent-2411-105: Since the original latent points are corrupted, we avoid perfect alignment by considering a fraction of latent points in the SCD distance (\( l^{\lambda _{cd \)).}}
elements.sent-2411-106: To compute \( l^{\lambda _{cd \), we sum the lowest \(\lambda\%\) of the sorted minimum squared distances between the two point sets, reducing the influence of outliers and focusing on coarse similarities.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, given that the initial shape latent $z_0$, obtained from the input point cloud, potentially leads to inaccurate guidance for the denoising network, we propose an adjustment over the shape latent using the gradient descent technique.
elements.sent-2411-108: The update formula is expressed as: \begin{equation}z_0 \leftarrow z_0 - \gamma \nabla{z_0} l^{\lambda}_{cd} \label{eq:shape_latent_updating}\end{equation}.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{The algorithm for the proposed 3DD-TTA.} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Corrupted point cloud $\tilde{x}$, shape encoder $q_z(.)$, latent point encoder $q_h(.)$, decoder $p_d(.)$, diffusion prior $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_h(.)$, and source classifier $p_c(.)$ \State ${z}_0 \sim q_{z}\left(z_0 \mid \tilde{x}\right)$ \Comment{obtain the shape latent} \State ${h}_0 \sim q_{h}\left(h_0 \mid {z}_0, \tilde{x}\right)$ \Comment{obtain the latent points} \State ${h}_{t_w} \gets \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t_w}} \, {h}_{0} + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t_w}} \boldsymbol{\epsilon} $ \Comment{perturb the latent points} \State for $t \gets t_w ... 1$ do: \State $\, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \gets \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_h ({h}_{t}, z_0, t)$ \Comment{estimate the diffusion noise by $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_h(.)$} \State \, \quad $\bar{h}_0 \gets \frac{{h}_{t}-\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t}}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}}$ \Comment{estimate original latent points} \State \, \quad $l^{\lambda}_{cd}=\operatorname{CD}^{\lambda}(\bar{h}_0, {h}_0)$ \Comment{compute SCD distance} \State $\, \quad {z}_0 \gets {z}_0 - \gamma \nabla_{{z}_0} l^{\lambda}_{cd}$ \Comment{update the shape latent} \State $\, \quad {h}_{t-1} \gets \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \bar{h}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t-\eta \nabla_{{h}_t}l^{\lambda}_{cd}$ \Comment{obtain the latent points for previous time step} \State $x \gets p_d(x|{z}_0, {h}_0)$ \Comment{reconstruct the point cloud} \State ${c} \sim p_c({c}|x)$ \Comment{pass the point cloud to the source classifier} \end{algorithmic} \label{algorithm1}
elements.sent-2411-109: This gradient descent-based updating technique ensures that the shape latent is adjusted in a direction that enhances the denoising process, resulting in improved alignment between the denoised and the original latent points.
elements.sent-2411-110: We iteratively apply the denoising steps following the update rules in Eq. \ref{eq:latent_point_updating and \ref{eq:shape_latent_updating .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: This process is repeated up to $t_w$ times to approximate $h_0$ and $z_0$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Finally, we decode the denoised latent points using the decoder: ${x \sim p_{d}\left({x \mid {z _0, {\mathbf{h _0\right)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The reconstructed point cloud $x$ is then fed into the source classifier $p_c$ to determine the final class label: $c \sim p_c(x)$.

elements.sent-2411-114: The proposed 3DD-TTA method is summarized in Algorithm~\ref{algorithm1 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Extensive experiments show that our adaptation method effectively restores corrupted point clouds to the source domain while remaining agnostic to corruption types, significantly improving the performance of the source classifier.
elements.sent-2411-116: Setup: We adopted the hierarchical latent diffusion model proposed in \cite{lion , pre-trained on the ShapeNet dataset \cite{shapenet for our 3DD-TTA model.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We employed the deterministic DDIM \cite{ddim process over 100 total time steps to accelerate the denoising process.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Our investigation reveals that perturbing the latent points with noise corresponding to the 5th time step and denoising through these five steps is sufficient to mitigate most of the corruption.
elements.sent-2411-119: For the weights used in updating the shape latent ($\gamma$) and latent points ($\eta$), we conducted a grid search and determined that 0.01 is the optimal setting for both, after testing various configurations.
elements.sent-2411-120: Regarding the SCD distance parameter ($\lambda$), we analyzed different values and found that $\lambda = 0.96$ yields the best performance (refer to Section B in the appendix for more details).
elements.sent-2411-121: All experiments were conducted on a single NVIDIA A6000 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-122: ShapeNet-c: ShapeNet \cite{shapenet , a large-scale point cloud dataset with 51,127 shapes across 55 categories, is used to train the LION model \cite{lion , the backbone of our 3DD-TTA framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Thus, we primarily evaluate our TTA model's performance on this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-124: For experiments, we applied 15 types of corruptions to the test set using the open-source ModelNet40-c implementation \cite{modelnet40-c , creating a corrupted version called ShapeNet-c.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Further details about the corruption function can be found in Appendix A.
elements.sent-2411-126: ModelNet40-c: ModelNet40-c \cite{modelnet40-c introduces the aforementioned 15 typical corruptions to the original test set of ModelNet40 \cite{modelnet40 .}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We use this dataset to test the generalization power of the proposed 3DD-TTA approach on datasets beyond the source datasets.
elements.sent-2411-128: ScanObjectNN-c: ScanObjectNN \cite{scanobjectnn , a real-world point cloud dataset with 15 categories, is corrupted using the same open-source code as ModelNet40-c \cite{modelnet40-c , introducing 15 corruptions into the test set.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: We refer to this dataset as ScanObjectNN-c.
elements.sent-2411-130: We compared our 3DD-TTA model to the non-adapted source classifier, PointMAE (src) \cite{point-mae , and six TTA approaches originally proposed for images, each assuming access to a single sample for test-time adaptation.}
elements.sent-2411-131: The baselines include: (1) SHOT \cite{shot , which minimizes output entropy; (2) T3A \cite{t3a , which learns class-specific prototypes to replace the pre-trained classifier; (3) TENT \cite{tent , which also minimizes prediction entropy; (4) DUA \cite{dua , which updates batch normalization statistics to adapt at test time; (5) TTT-Rot \cite{ttt-rot , which adapts via self-supervised rotation prediction tasks; and (6) MATE-S \cite{mirza2023mate , which fine-tunes PointMAE \cite{point-mae for reconstruction at test time.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-132: Table \ref{table1-shapenet compares the performance of various TTA approaches on the ShapeNet-c dataset for different types of corruptions.}
elements.sent-2411-133: The table clearly shows that the 3DD-TTA method outperforms the other methods by a significant margin in most corruption types.
elements.sent-2411-134: In particular, 3DD-TTA excels at mitigating noise-related corruptions such as uniform, Gaussian, impulse, and background noise.
elements.sent-2411-135: This effectiveness is attributed to the denoising capabilities of DDMs during the reverse diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-136: Notably, 3DD-TTA dramatically boosts the source classifier's performance on background noise, raising accuracy from 15.0$\%$ to 77.6$\%$.
elements.sent-2411-137: In addition, our 3DD-TTA outperforms other TTA frameworks on density-based corruptions such as cut-out and density increase.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, the model faces limitations in addressing the transformation-based deformations like shear and rotation.
elements.sent-2411-139: This limitation is due to the training-free nature of the model, making it challenging to reverse transformations to their original shape without additional training.
elements.sent-2411-140: Figure \ref{fig:samples shows the qualitative performance of the 3DD-TTA algorithm in reconstructing the corrupted point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The model successfully restores the point clouds, thereby improving the performance of the source classifier.
elements.sent-2411-142: More qualitative results are provided in Appendix D.
elements.sent-2411-143: We suggest that our model, built on the LION backbone \cite{lion , which was pre-trained on the extensive ShapeNet dataset \cite{shapenet , can effectively generalize to reconstruct corrupted point clouds from other datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: To validate this, we conduct experiments on the ModelNet40-c and ScanObjectNN-c datasets.
elements.sent-2411-145: Table \ref{table 2-modelnet40-c presents the performance of various TTA models in addressing different corruptions within the ModelNet40-c dataset \cite{modelnet40-c .}}
elements.sent-2411-146: Notably, the diffusion model is effective in addressing the noise-related corruptions in ModelNet40-c dataset \cite{modelnet40-c , despite being trained on the ShapeNet dataset \cite{shapenet .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Similarly, the model outperforms other methods in addressing density-related corruptions but is less effective for transformation-based corruptions, ranking second or third for these deformations.
elements.sent-2411-148: We also conducted experiments on the corrupted version of the real-world ScanObjectNN dataset \cite{scanobjectnn , which inherently suffers from noise, background issues, and occlusion.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Table \ref{tab:table3 displays the performance of the 3DD-TTA model on this dataset, demonstrating its strong generalization capability to real-world datasets.}

elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figure/ablation-steps2.pdf} \caption{ (left) Accuracy of the source classifier after adaptation using different numbers of denoising steps. (right) Performance of the model across different numbers of total DDIM steps.} \label{fig:ablation-steps}
elements.sent-2411-150: Number of Denoising Steps for Reconstruction: While the denoising diffusion network in the original LION \cite{lion model has been trained with 1000 time steps, we posit that a total of 100 deterministic DDIM time steps is sufficient for sampling a point cloud from pure Gaussian noise without compromising point cloud quality.}
elements.sent-2411-151: This implies a 10-step skip for each DDIM step.
elements.sent-2411-152: We base our assumption on DDIMs' efficiency in achieving high-quality generation with fewer steps and the lack of high-frequency details in point cloud meshes, meaning that reducing denoising steps should not significantly impact the quality of the generated point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-153: As the first experiment, we fixed the total number of DDIM steps to 100 and investigated the optimal number of denoising steps for reconstructing corrupted point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-154: Our analysis, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:ablation-steps (left), demonstrates that five denoising steps are sufficient for most types of corruption.}
elements.sent-2411-155: However, for extreme outliers such as background noise, a more extensive denoising process of up to 35 steps is required.
elements.sent-2411-156: This is because outliers and isolated points in the point cloud require significant perturbation to fill the gaps with noise, followed by denoising to return the point cloud to its natural manifold.
elements.sent-2411-157: Adaptation Time vs. Number of Denoising Steps: Table \ref{tab:table4 shows the time duration required for test-time adaptation using our method as the number of denoising steps increases.}
elements.sent-2411-158: The adaptation time grows linearly with the number of denoising steps, ranging from 12 ms for 1 step to 290 ms for 40 steps.
elements.sent-2411-159: As only 5 denoising steps (taking 40 ms) are sufficient for most corruptions, the proposed method is efficient, making it suitable for time-sensitive applications.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Adaptation time (ms) for different numbers of denoising steps in the 3DD-TTA method.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.9pt} 1.3{ \begin{tabular}{lccccccc} \multicolumn{1}{l}{$\#$ Denoising Steps: } 1 5 10 20 30 40 Time Duration (ms) 12 40 74 142 218 290 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:table4}
elements.sent-2411-160: Number of Total DDIM Steps: In a second experiment, we investigated the impact of the total number of DDIM steps to validate our assumption of 100 steps.
elements.sent-2411-161: We chose the background noise for this analysis because it requires more denoising steps to resolve, thereby better illustrating the impact of reducing DDIM steps.
elements.sent-2411-162: We fixed 35 denoising steps for reconstruction with 100 total DDIM steps, then varied the total DDIM steps (S) from 0 to 1000, proportionally adjusting the denoising steps to 35*(S/100) to assess its impact on source classifier performance.
elements.sent-2411-163: Figure \ref{fig:ablation-steps (right) shows that accuracy remains stable with fewer DDIM steps until around 100 steps, after which a sharp decline occurs.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Based on this, we selected 100 DDIM steps to balance adaptation time and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-165: Limitation: Our model performs exceptionally well with just five denoising steps for most types of corruption, making it efficient and suitable for time-sensitive applications.
elements.sent-2411-166: However, severe outliers, such as background noise, may require up to 35 steps, increasing the processing time for these cases.
elements.sent-2411-167: Additionally, future work could explore determining the optimal number of denoising steps adaptively based on the input point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-168: In addition, while the model effectively addresses density inconsistencies and noise-based corruptions, it struggles with transformation-based deformations like rotation and shear.
elements.sent-2411-169: Making parts of the network trainable could help handle both types of corruption in the future.
elements.sent-2411-170: This paper introduces a novel approach to 3D point cloud test-time adaptation, addressing the challenge of managing discrepancies between training and testing samples, especially in corrupted point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-171: Unlike existing methods that often fine-tune pre-trained models using self-supervised learning, the proposed method focuses on adapting input point cloud samples to the source domain through a denoising diffusion strategy.
elements.sent-2411-172: By leveraging a diffusion model trained on the source domain, the approach iteratively denoises the perturbed test point clouds, enhancing their consistency with the source domain without adjusting the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-173: Incorporating the proposed updating strategy based on the gradient of the Selective Chamfer Distance (SCD) ensures the generation of high-fidelity, noise-free test samples.
elements.sent-2411-174: Extensive experiments conducted on ShapeNet \cite{shapenet , ModelNet40 \cite{modelnet40 , and ScanObjectNN \cite{scanobjectnn datasets validate the efficacy of the proposed approach, demonstrating new state-of-the-art results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: This innovative method not only advances the field of 3D test-time adaptation but also provides a promising direction for handling real-world challenges in various domains.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: If you are submitting a paper to the second round for a paper that received a Revise decision in the first round, please use this template to address the reviewer concerns and highlight the changes made since the original submission.
elements.sent-2411-177: The rebuttal is limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-179: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-180: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-181: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-182: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-183: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-184: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-185: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-186: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-187: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-189: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-191: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-192: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-193: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-194: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-195: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-196: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in Figure \ref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-197: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-198: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.

elements.sent-2411-203: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
